coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly graphene quantum dots as a novel spectrofluorimetric probe for lamivudine quantification with evaluation of its greenness and blueness profiles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Alqahtani, Ali
Alqahtani, Taha
Serag, Ahmed
coredata.dc:description: In this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were employed for quantitatively analyzing lamivudine using a fluorescence quenching technique. This approach allows for sensitive determination of the concentration of lamivudine in different matrices without requiring derivatization. The mechanism behind the fluorescence intensity quenching between GQDs and lamivudine molecules was explored using the Stern Volmer equation, revealing dynamic quenching behavior. Additionally, various factors affecting fluorescence quenching efficiency such as pH, GQDs concentration, and incubation time were carefully tuned. Moreover, our developed method successfully met ICH guidelines for validation parameters including linearity, accuracy, precision, and selectivity demonstrating excellent performance. The results showed good accuracy and precision, with a mean recovery value of 101.91% for method accuracy and a relative standard deviation of 0.682 and 1.489 for intraday and interday precision, respectively. Finally, the greenness and blueness of the developed method were also investigated to assess its environmental friendliness and analytical practicality. Greenness evaluation using the AGREE tool demonstrated that the developed method has a low environmental impact with an AGREE score of 0.75, Besides, the blueness evaluating using the BAGI tool indicated that the developed method is practical, reliable, and well-suited for routine analysis of lamivudine in various samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Lamivudine
Quenching
AGREE
BAGI

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Validation of the developed GQDs probe for detecting lamivudine
Analysis procedure for determining lamivudine concentration in the pharmaceutical dosage form
Analysis procedure for determining lamivudine concentration in spiked human plasma samples
Mechanism of lamivudine sensing using GQDs
Optimization of the experimental conditions for lamivudine sensing using GQDs
Validation of the sensing method for lamivudine quantification
Application of the developed method for analyzing lamivudine in pharmaceutical formulation and spiked plasma samples
Greenness and blueness assessment of the proposed method for lamivudine quantification

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: King Khalid University Deanship of Scientific Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University grant number) RGP2/405/44 (.
The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through large group Research Project under grant number RGP2/405/44.
Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University grant number (RGP2/405/44).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of the GQDs showing a potent emission at a λmax of 435 nm when excited at 350 nm.

. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra resulting from the quenching process of GQDs with various lamivudine concentrations, and (B) Stern–Volmer chart demonstrating the interaction between lamivudine and the developed GQDs at different temperatures.

. Investigation of experimental factors impacting the stability of fluorescence quenching of GQDs by lamivudine, such as (A) pH of the buffer, (B) volume of the buffer, (C) quantity of the GQDs, and (D) duration of incubation. The error bars illustrate the standard deviation derived from three independent measurements.

. The competitive selectivity experiments showing the fluorescence quenching factor [(F0-F)/F0] responses of the developed GQDs interaction with lamivudine and various interfering compounds in addition to plasma samples.

. The assessment of the (A) greenness of the developed method using the AGREE tool to determine its environmental impact and (B) blueness of the developed method using the BAGI tool to determine its analytical practicability.
Validation data for determination of lamivudine by the developed GQDs fluorescent probe.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured on the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured in the three consecutive days.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitative analysis of lamivudine in the commercial Lamidine® tablets by the proposed method and statistical comparison with the reported method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in parenthesis are tabulated values of “t “and “F” at (P = 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Accuracy and precision of plasma samples using the developed GQDs fluorescent probe for lamivudine analysis.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly graphene quantum dots as a novel spectrofluorimetric probe for lamivudine quantification with evaluation of its greenness and blueness profiles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Cairo 11751, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Cairo 11751, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were employed for quantitatively analyzing lamivudine using a fluorescence quenching technique. This approach allows for sensitive determination of the concentration of lamivudine in different matrices without requiring derivatization. The mechanism behind the fluorescence intensity quenching between GQDs and lamivudine molecules was explored using the Stern Volmer equation, revealing dynamic quenching behavior. Additionally, various factors affecting fluorescence quenching efficiency such as pH, GQDs concentration, and incubation time were carefully tuned. Moreover, our developed method successfully met ICH guidelines for validation parameters including linearity, accuracy, precision, and selectivity demonstrating excellent performance. The results showed good accuracy and precision, with a mean recovery value of 101.91% for method accuracy and a relative standard deviation of 0.682 and 1.489 for intraday and interday precision, respectively. Finally, the greenness and blueness of the developed method were also investigated to assess its environmental friendliness and analytical practicality. Greenness evaluation using the AGREE tool demonstrated that the developed method has a low environmental impact with an AGREE score of 0.75, Besides, the blueness evaluating using the BAGI tool indicated that the developed method is practical, reliable, and well-suited for routine analysis of lamivudine in various samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lamivudine, also known as 3TC, is an antiretroviral medication used to prevent and treat HIV/AIDS [1]. It belongs to the class of nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors and works by inhibiting the reverse transcriptase enzyme, thus preventing the replication of the HIV virus [2]. Lamivudine is available both by itself and together with zidovudine or abacavir [3,4]. Monitoring the concentration of lamivudine in patients undergoing antiretroviral therapy is crucial for assessing treatment effectiveness and optimizing dosing regimens. Hence, several analytical methods have been developed for the quantitative analysis of lamivudine in commercial and biological samples. High-performance liquid chromatography using reversed-phase stationary phases coupled with UV or tandem mass spectrometry detection is the most widely used technique for analyzing lamivudine concentrations [5–8]. Other methods, such as thin layer chromatography and micellar electrokinetic chromatography combined with UV detection, have also been employed for this purpose [9–11]. Furthermore, UV spectrophotometric methods assisted by signal processing techniques such as continuous wavelet transform and chemometric models such as PLS have also been explored to quantify lamivudine in combination with other antiviral drugs [12,13]. Additionally, electrochemical methods like differential pulse and square wave voltammetry have been utilized for determining lamivudine in pharmaceutical dosage forms and human serum [14].

However, these previously described methods often require complex sample preparation procedures, have limited sensitivity, and may exhibit interference from other co-administered drugs in addition to their high environmental impact. To overcome these limitations, there is a need to develop alternative methods that are simple, sensitive, and environmentally friendly. One potential approach is the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the quantitative analysis of pharmaceutical compounds [15,16]. GQDs are nanoscale carbon-based materials that possess unique optical and electronic properties [17]. They exhibit a high surface area, good photostability, and strong fluorescence properties, making them suitable for various applications in analytical chemistry [18]. In addition, they are considered eco-friendly, posing their potential as an attractive alternative to traditional analytical methods [19]. For example, nitrogen doped GQDs have been successfully utilized for the quantitative analysis of gabapentin, and risperidone in pharmaceutical formulations and in biological fluids posing as a promising alternative to conventional methods of derivatization non-fluorogenic compounds for fluorescence-based analysis [19,20].

To the best of our knowledge there is no previous research on the use of GQDs for the quantitative analysis of lamivudine using spectrofluorimetric techniques. Hence, the aim of the present study is to investigate the use of GQDs for the quantitative analysis of lamivudine in its commercial and biological samples by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs when they are quenched in the presence of lamivudine. Optimization of sensing conditions such as the concentration of GQDs, pH of the solution, and reaction time will be conducted to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of this method. Moreover, the ecological impact of using GQDs for the analysis of lamivudine will be assessed using the Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) tool [21] and the Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) [22] to evaluate the environmental greenness and analytical practicability of the proposed method.

The following materials, reagents, and standard solutions were used in the experiment: GQDs were provided by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) and were used as received. Lamivudine with a certified purity of 99.78 % was kindly supplied by the Egyptian drug authority (EDA). The commercial sample of lamivudine (Lamidine ® tablets) was manufactured by: EVA PHARMA for Pharmaceuticals & Medical Appliances claimed to contain 150 mg lamivudine per tablet and purchased from a local pharmacy in Cairo, Egypt. All additional solvents and reagents used in the experiment were of a high analytical purity and sourced from El Nasr Company. Freshly distilled water was utilized for the process. Different buffer solutions with varying pH values were prepared according to the recommendations specified in the US Pharmacopoeia.

A standard lamivudine solution was made with a concentration of 100 μg/mL by dissolving 10 mg of lamivudine in 40 mL ethanol and then adjusting the volume to 100 mL with distilled water. A working solution of lamivudine at a concentration of 20 μg/mL was then prepared by diluting the stock solution appropriately with distilled water.

A Jasco fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-6200, Japan) was utilized to measure the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs. The acquisition parameters were as follows: excitation wavelength of 350 nm and emission wavelengths ranged from 390 to 470 nm. These measurements were performed using a 1 cm quartz cuvette, and the data was recorded and analyzed with Spectra Manager software. Both the excitation and emission monochromator slit widths were set to 5 nm. A Shimadzu UV–visible 1800 spectrophotometer was employed to measure the absorption spectra in the UV–visible range.

To construct the calibration curve, a series of standard solutions with varying concentrations of lamivudine ranging from 0.5 to 6 µg/mL were prepared. Each solution was created by combining specific volumes of the working solution with separate 10 mL volumetric flasks pre-filled with 1 mL of GQDs solution and 1.5 mL of borate buffer at pH 8. After an incubation period of 5 min, the solutions were thoroughly mixed, and their final volume was adjusted using double distilled water. The fluorescence signals (F) were subsequently determined using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission wavelength of 435 nm. Furthermore, a blank sample was prepared under the same conditions and its fluorescence level (F0) was noted. The calibration graph was created by plotting (F0/F) against the drug concentrations in µg/mL derived from these measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the developed GQDs probe for detecting lamivudine
ce:section.ce:para: Validation of the GQDs sensor to detect lamivudine was carried out in compliance with ICH guidelines [23]. Linearity was examined using different concentrations of lamivudine, and the resulting calibration curve yielded values for correlation coefficient (R2), slope, and y-intercept. Limits of detection and quantification were determined based on the standard deviation of the response calculated from residuals and slope of the calibration curve. Accuracy and precision were assessed by comparing measured concentrations with known concentrations through replicates at three concentration levels: 1, 3, and 5 μg/mL. Precision was evaluated by determining relative standard deviation (RSD%) within specified conditions either on the same day or over three consecutive days. Selectivity testing involved measuring response in presence of potentially interfering substances found in its formulation as well as plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis procedure for determining lamivudine concentration in the pharmaceutical dosage form
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the lamivudine content in a pharmaceutical dosage form, ten Lamidine® tablets were accurately weighed and powdered. Then, an amount of powder equivalent to 10 mg of lamivudine was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask and dissolved in a 40 mL of ethanol under sonication for 10 min. The volume was then made up to mark with water, and the solution was filtered using a syringe filter. The filtered solution was then diluted appropriately to obtain a working concentration of 20 μg/mL. The same methodology was implemented as previously outlined to establish the calibration graph with the working solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the sample solutions was assessed using the same excitation and emission wavelengths mentioned earlier. The concentration of lamivudine in tablets was then calculated from the calibration curve by employing the obtained regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis procedure for determining lamivudine concentration in spiked human plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the concentration of lamivudine in spiked human plasma samples, a standard procedure was followed. This involved spiking the samples with different amounts of a lamivudine standard solution (20 μg/mL), protein precipitation using 5 mL acetonitrile, centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 20 min, and subsequent filtration using a syringe filter. The filtered plasma samples were then dried and reconstituted with 1 mL of ethanol before being transferred to 10 mL volumetric flasks for analysis using the same fluorescence intensity measurement method as described earlier. The concentration of lamivudine in the spiked human plasma samples was determined by comparing the fluorescence intensity of the samples to the calibration curve obtained from the corresponding regression equation.

The spectral characteristics of GQDs were analyzed at different excitation and emission wavelengths. The excitation wavelengths ranged from 200 nm to 400 nm, while the emission wavelengths ranged from 400 nm to 700 nm. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for measuring the fluorescence intensity of GQDs were determined to be 350 nm and 435 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A). These excitation and emission wavelengths were found to provide the highest fluorescence intensity for GQDs, indicating their suitability for quantitative analysis of lamivudine. Although no red or blue shifts were observed in the fluorescence spectra of GQDs when different excitation wavelengths were used, these GQDs were mainly affected by the pH of the solution (Fig. 1B). This could be attributed to the presence of functional groups on the surface of the GQDs such as –OH groups, which can interact with protons in the solution and alter their spectral characteristics. Hence, careful tuning of the pH of the solution is necessary to ensure accurate and reliable measurements of lamivudine concentration using GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of lamivudine sensing using GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching of fluorescence intensity of GQDs by lamivudine was investigated to understand the mechanism of lamivudine sensing using GQDs. Upon increasing the concentration of lamivudine, a corresponding decrease in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was observed (Fig. 2 A). Generally, there are three possible mechanisms of quenching: static quenching, dynamic quenching, and the inner filter effect. Upon examination of the UV spectrum of lamivudine no significant absorbance was observed in the excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs (350 nm and 435 nm), ruling out the possibility of inner filter effect [24]. Hence, it can be concluded that the quenching of fluorescence intensity of GQDs by lamivudine is likely due to either static or dynamic quenching. A temperature dependence study was conducted to determine the nature of the quenching mechanism (Fig. 2B). The results, using the Stern Volmer equation, showed that the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched by lamivudine in a dynamic manner. This could be attributed to an increase in diffusion coefficient and energy transfer between lamivudine and GQDs as temperature rises, indicating a dynamic quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for lamivudine sensing using GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the experimental conditions for lamivudine sensing using GQDs, several factors were investigated. These factors included the pH of the solution, concentration of GQDs, and incubation time. The pH of the solution was found to have a significant impact on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs by lamivudine. Slightly alkaline conditions (pH 8–9) were found to be optimal for the detection of lamivudine using GQDs while acidic conditions resulted in decreased sensitivity (Fig. 3 A&B). This is because the surface of GQDs contains functional groups such as –OH, which can interact with protons in the solution and affect the spectral properties of GQDs lamivudine complex. Moreover, higher alkaline conditions also resulted in lower stability of the GQDs lamivudine complex, leading to decreased sensitivity. Additionally, the concentration of GQDs in the solution was found to have an impact on the fluorescence quenching. Higher concentrations of GQDs led to higher fluorescence quenching of lamivudine, indicating a more sensitive detection. It was found that 1 mL solution of GQDs provided the optimal concentration for detecting lamivudine (Fig. 3C). Another factor that was investigated was the incubation time of the GQDs with lamivudine. Interestingly, it was found that longer incubation times resulted in higher fluorescence quenching of lamivudine with 5 min of incubation being the optimal time for sensitive detection of lamivudine (Fig. 3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the sensing method for lamivudine quantification
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the sensing method for lamivudine quantification, a calibration curve was constructed using different concentrations of lamivudine. The curve showed an excellent linear relationship between the concentration of lamivudine and the fluorescence intensity quenching of GQDs with an R2 value of 0.9997 within a range of 0.5–6 µg/mL (Table 1 ). The limit of detection and quantification were calculated based on the residual standard deviation, resulting in values of 0.1090 and 0.3304 µg/mL, respectively. This indicates that the developed sensing method using GQDs is highly sensitive and capable of accurately quantifying lamivudine in the micro to nanogram per milliliter range.

Accuracy and precision of the sensing method were assessed by analyzing the recovery and the standard deviation of known concentrations of lamivudine (Table 1). The results showed good accuracy and precision, with a mean recovery value of 101.91 ± 0.758 % for method accuracy and a relative standard deviation of 0.682 and 1.489 for intraday and interday precision, respectively.

The selectivity of the sensing method was evaluated by examining the interference from other common excipients found in the formulation of this antiretroviral therapy such as lactose, magnesium stearate, and microcrystalline cellulose. The results showed that the presence of these excipients did not significantly interfere with the detection of lamivudine using GQDs (Fig. 4 ). Moreover, the selectivity of this methods was further confirmed by analyzing pooled plasma samples, the results indicate that no interference from endogenous components in the plasma affected the quantification of lamivudine using GQDs posing its further applicability in analyzing lamivudine in biological and clinical samples (Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the developed method for analyzing lamivudine in pharmaceutical formulation and spiked plasma samples
Greenness and blueness assessment of the proposed method for lamivudine quantification
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the greenness and blueness of the proposed method for lamivudine quantification using GQDs, several factors were considered. These factors included the use of GQDs, which are a green and sustainable material, as well as the minimal use of reagents and energy in the analysis process.

The greenness of the developed method was evaluated using the AGREE tool, which assesses the environmental impact of a chemical procedure. The AGREE tool indicated that the developed method had a high level of greenness with an overall value of 0.75 (Fig. 5 A), indicating its eco-friendly nature. The pros of our method lies, as evident by the AGREE analysis, in its use of biocompatible and non-toxic material (GQDs), minimal reagent consumption, and low energy requirements for the spectrofluorimetric device. In addition, several other aspects contribute to the greenness of the method, including the high throughput of the analysis process, the absence of derivatization steps, and the minimal sample preparation stages.

The blue applicability grade index (BAGI) of the method refers to its suitability for a wide range of applications and its potential impact on the field of analytical chemistry. The results of this assessment indicated a high blue applicability grade index for the proposed method with a value of 70 (Fig. 5B) highlighting its potential impact and broad applicability in the field of analytical chemistry. Among the different factors aiding in the high blue applicability grade index are the simplicity of the analytical technique employed, readily available materials (GQDs), and the high throughput number of samples per hour that can be analyzed using the developed method.

In conclusion, this study demonstrates the potential use of GQDs for quantitative analysis of antiretroviral therapy lamivudine. The use of GQDs as a fluorescent probe allowed for the quenching of their fluorescence intensity, providing a reliable and sensitive method for lamivudine quantification. A dynamic quenching pattern was observed for the GQDs in the presence of lamivudine, indicating a strong interaction between the two molecules. This interaction was utilized to develop a spectrofluorimetric method for quantitative analysis of lamivudine. Various factors affecting experimental conditions such as pH, GQDs concentration, and reaction time were optimized to achieve maximum sensitivity and accuracy in the analysis. The developed method exhibited excellent analytical performance with a wide linear range of lamivudine concentrations and low limits of detection and quantification. The proposed method using GQDs offers several advantages, including its greenness, high throughput, and broad applicability in analytical chemistry i.e. blueness providing an efficient solution for analyzing lamivudine in various samples. Overall, this research highlights the potential of GQDs as a promising tool for quantitative analysis of antiretroviral therapy lamivudine in real pharmaceutical and biological samples, which can contribute to the advancement of antiretroviral therapy monitoring and optimization.
Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University grant number (RGP2/405/44).

Ali Alqahtani: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Taha Alqahtani: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ahmed Serag: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through large group Research Project under grant number RGP2/405/44.

Mosleh, Molecularly imprinted polymer containing fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a new fluorescent nanosensor for detection of methamphetamine, Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy, 229 (2020) 118021.
